questions.question: Explain the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs and how LSTM addresses it.
keyPoints: Vanishing gradient occurs when gradients become extremely small during backpropagation through time
Prevents learning of long-term dependencies in sequences
LSTM uses gating mechanisms (especially cell state) that allow gradients to flow unchanged
The additive nature of cell state updates preserves gradients over many time steps

questions.question: Describe the three gates in LSTM and their functions.
keyPoints: Forget gate: decides what information to discard from previous cell state (output 0-1)
Input gate: decides what new information to add to cell state
Output gate: decides what parts of cell state to output
All gates use sigmoid activation for flow control (0-1 range)

questions.question: What is the role of the cell state in LSTM architecture?
keyPoints: Acts as the memory of the network
Runs through entire LSTM chain carrying information with minimal changes
Information can be added or removed via gates
Enables long-term dependencies to be learned effectively

questions.question: Explain the difference between LSTM and GRU architectures.
keyPoints: LSTM has 3 gates (input, forget, output) plus separate cell and hidden states
GRU has 2 gates (update, reset) and combines cell state with hidden state
GRU is computationally faster and has fewer parameters
LSTM is more expressive and better for complex long-term dependencies

questions.question: What is Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in the context of LSTM training?
keyPoints: Training algorithm that unrolls the LSTM through all time steps
Computes gradients by going backward through the unfolded network
Gradients flow back through cell state enabling learning of long dependencies
Can face exploding/vanishing gradient issues addressed by gradient clipping

questions.question: Describe bidirectional LSTM and its advantages.
keyPoints: Uses two separate LSTM layers processing sequence in opposite directions
One layer processes forward, one processes backward
Combines outputs to get context from both past and future
Useful when entire sequence is available (e.g., NLP tasks)

questions.question: List four common applications of LSTM networks.
keyPoints: Machine translation and text generation
Speech recognition and voice search
Time series prediction and forecasting
Sentiment analysis and text classification

questions.question: Draw and explain the complete LSTM architecture with equations for each gate. How does the forward pass work step by step?
keyPoints: Diagram showing cell state, hidden state, and three gates
Forget gate equation: f(t) = σ(Wf·[h(t-1), x(t)] + bf)
Input gate equation: i(t) = σ(Wi·[h(t-1), x(t)] + bi), C̃(t) = tanh(Wc·[h(t-1), x(t)] + bc)
Cell state update: C(t) = f(t)×C(t-1) + i(t)×C̃(t)
Output gate: o(t) = σ(Wo·[h(t-1), x(t)] + bo), h(t) = o(t)×tanh(C(t))
Step-by-step flow explanation

questions.question: Explain the vanishing gradient problem in detail. How does LSTM architecture specifically solve this problem compared to standard RNNs? Include mathematical reasoning.
keyPoints: Vanishing gradient: gradients multiply through many time steps, becoming exponentially small
Standard RNN: Wh recurrence matrix eigenvalues < 1 cause gradient decay
LSTM solution: cell state provides additive path for gradients (no multiplicative factors)
Gradient flow through cell state: ∂C(t)/∂C(t-1) = f(t) (close to 1)
Gates learn what to remember/forget adaptively
Mathematical proof showing gradient doesn't vanish through cell state

questions.question: Compare LSTM with standard RNN and GRU. Discuss their architectures, advantages, disadvantages, and suitable use cases for each.
keyPoints: Standard RNN: simple recurrence, suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients
LSTM: 3 gates, cell state + hidden state, best for complex long dependencies
GRU: 2 gates, combined state, faster training, fewer parameters
Complexity: LSTM > GRU > RNN in both parameters and computation
Use cases: LSTM for translation/speech, GRU for mobile/embedded, standard RNN for simple sequences

questions.question: What are peephole connections in LSTM? Explain their purpose and how they modify the standard LSTM architecture. Also discuss variants like coupled input-forget gate LSTM.
keyPoints: Peephole connections: gates can view cell state before making decisions
Standard LSTM: gates only see previous hidden state and current input
Peephole addition: each gate equation includes cell state term
Coupled input-forget gate: instead of two separate gates, use 1 - forget to determine input
Variants aim to reduce parameters and improve gradient flow
